paragraph.content: No data available for this section

content.content: Result Comment: NOT DETECTED generally indicates the absence of target nucleic acid but does not exclude the presence of target nucleic acid at levels below test sensitivity, or inhibitory substances infrequently present in clinical specimens.

Interpretive Data: Transmission based precautions for a detected result:
*** Droplet + Contact Precautions required for seven (7) days from symptom onset AND 72 hours after resolution of fever and respiratory signs and symptoms – whichever is later. ***
Transmission based precautions for a not detected or invalid result:
***Standard Precautions required, including mask and eye protection when patient is symptomatic with respiratory infection presentation. Per organizational expectations during COVID-19 response – medical mask and eye protection are required for all patient interactions. ***
Assay Information:
This assay was performed using the FDA-approved Alinity m Resp-4-Plex assay, a multiplex real-time reverse transcription (RT) polymerase chain reaction (PCR) test intended for the simultaneous qualitative detection and differentiation of RNA from SARS-CoV-2, influenza A virus (flu A), influenza B virus (flu B), and/or Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) in upper respiratory specimens. This assay has been validated by Virginia Mason Clinical Microbiology Laboratory, a CLIA high-complexity clinical laboratory, for in vitro diagnostic use under FDA Emergency Use Authorization only.

Result Comment: NOT DETECTED generally indicates the absence of target nucleic acid but does not exclude the presence of target nucleic acid at levels below test sensitivity, or inhibitory substances infrequently present in clinical specimens.

Interpretive Data: Transmission based precautions for a detected result:
*** Droplet + Contact Precautions required for seven (7) days from symptom onset AND 72 hours after resolution of fever and respiratory signs and symptoms – whichever is later. ***
Transmission based precautions for a not detected or invalid result:
***Standard Precautions required, including mask and eye protection when patient is symptomatic with respiratory infection presentation. Per organizational expectations during COVID-19 response – medical mask and eye protection are required for all patient interactions. ***
Assay Information:
This assay was performed using the FDA-approved Alinity m Resp-4-Plex assay, a multiplex real-time reverse transcription (RT) polymerase chain reaction (PCR) test intended for the simultaneous qualitative detection and differentiation of RNA from SARS-CoV-2, influenza A virus (flu A), influenza B virus (flu B), and/or Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) in upper respiratory specimens. This assay has been validated by Virginia Mason Clinical Microbiology Laboratory, a CLIA high-complexity clinical laboratory, for in vitro diagnostic use under FDA Emergency Use Authorization only.
Result Comment: DETECTED result indicates the presence of RSV. Clinical correlation with patient history and other diagnostic information is necessary to determine patient infection status.

Interpretive Data: Transmission based precautions for a detected result:
*** Droplet + Contact Precautions required for seven (7) days from symptom onset AND 72 hours after resolution of fever and respiratory signs and symptoms – whichever is later. ***
Transmission based precautions for a not detected or invalid result:
***Standard Precautions required, including mask and eye protection when patient is symptomatic with respiratory infection presentation. Per organizational expectations during COVID-19 response – medical mask and eye protection are required for all patient interactions. ***
Assay Information:
This assay was performed using the FDA-approved Alinity m Resp-4-Plex assay, a multiplex real-time reverse transcription (RT) polymerase chain reaction (PCR) test intended for the simultaneous qualitative detection and differentiation of RNA from SARS-CoV-2, influenza A virus (flu A), influenza B virus (flu B), and/or Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) in upper respiratory specimens. This assay has been validated by Virginia Mason Clinical Microbiology Laboratory, a CLIA high-complexity clinical laboratory, for in vitro diagnostic use under FDA Emergency Use Authorization only.

Result Comment: NOT DETECTED result does not preclude COVID-19 and should not be used as the sole basis for patient management decisions. Non detected result must be combined with clinical observations, patient history, and epidemiological information.

Interpretive Data: Transmission based precautions for a detected result:
***Special Droplet + Contact Precautions required. For discontinuation, refer to current Virginia Mason Standard Work.***
Transmission based precautions for a not detected or invalid result:
***Standard Precautions required, including mask and eye protection when patient is symptomatic with respiratory infection presentation. Per organizational expectations during COVID-19 response – medical mask and eye protection are required for all patient interactions. ***
Assay Information:
This assay was performed using the FDA-approved Alinity m Resp-4-Plex assay, a multiplex real-time reverse transcription (RT) polymerase chain reaction (PCR) test intended for the simultaneous qualitative detection and differentiation of RNA from SARS-CoV-2, influenza A virus (flu A), influenza B virus (flu B), and/or Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) in upper respiratory specimens. This assay has been validated by Virginia Mason Clinical Microbiology Laboratory, a CLIA high-complexity clinical laboratory, for in vitro diagnostic use under FDA Emergency Use Authorization only.

text.paragraph:
Laboratory Information
Virginia Mason Medical Center Laboratory
CLIA Number: 50D0664403
1100 9th Avenue
Seattle, WA 98101-2756 US
No data available for this section
No data available for this section
No data available for this section

Respiratory Syncytial Virus, Pediatric
Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) infection is a common infection that occurs in childhood. RSV is similar to viruses that cause the common cold and the flu. RSV infection often is the cause of a condition known as bronchiolitis. This is a condition that causes inflammation of the air passages in the lungs (bronchioles).
RSV infection is often the reason that babies are brought to the hospital. This infection:
• Spreads very easily from person to person (is very contagious).
• Can make children sick again even if they have had it before.
• Usually affects children within the first 3 years of life but can occur at any age.
What are the causes?
This condition is caused by contact with RSV. The virus spreads through droplets from coughs and sneezes (respiratory secretions). Your child can catch it by:
• Having respiratory secretions on his or her hands and then touching his or her mouth, nose, or eyes.
• Breathing in respiratory secretions from, or coming in close physical contact with, someone who has this infection.
• Touching something that has been exposed to the virus (is contaminated) and then touching his or her mouth, nose, or eyes.
What increases the risk?
Your child may be more likely to develop severe breathing problems from RVS if he or she:
• Is younger than 2 years old.
• Was born early (prematurely).
• Was born with heart or lung disease, Down syndrome, or other medical problems that are long-term (chronic).
RVS infections are most common from the months of November to April. But they can happen any time of year.
What are the signs or symptoms?
Symptoms of this condition include:
• Breathing loudly (wheezing).
• Having brief pauses in breathing during sleep (apnea).
• Having shortness of breath.
• Coughing often.
• Having difficulty breathing.
• Having a runny nose.
• Having a fever.
• Wanting to eat less or being less active than usual.
• Having irritated eyes.
How is this diagnosed?
This condition is diagnosed based on your child's medical history and a physical exam. Your child may have tests, such as:
• A test of nasal discharge to check for RSV.
• A chest X-ray. This may be done if your child develops difficulty breathing.
• Blood tests to check for infection and dehydration getting worse.
How is this treated?
The goal of treatment is to lessen symptoms and support healing. Because RSV is a virus, usually no antibiotic medicine is prescribed. Your child may be given a medicine (bronchodilator) to open up airways in his or her lungs to help with breathing.
If your child has severe RSV infection or other health problems, he or she may need to go to the hospital. If your child:
• Is dehydrated, he or she may be given IV fluids.
• Develops breathing problems, oxygen may be given.
Follow these instructions at home:
Medicines
• Give over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your child's health care provider.
• Do not give your child aspirin because of the association with Reye's syndrome.
• Use salt–water (saline) nose drops to help keep your child's nose clear.
Lifestyle
• Keep your child away from smoke to avoid making breathing problems worse. Babies exposed to people's smoke are more likely to develop RSV.
General instructions
• Use a suction bulb as directed to remove nasal discharge and help relieve stuffed-up (congested) nose.
• Use a cool mist vaporizer in your child's bedroom at night. This is a machine that adds moisture to dry air. It helps loosen mucus.
• Have your child drink enough fluids to keep his or her urine pale yellow. Fast and heavy breathing can cause dehydration.
• Watch your child carefully and do not delay seeking medical care for any problems. Your child's condition can change quickly.
• Have your child return to his or her normal activities as told by his or her health care provider. Ask your child's health care provider what activities are safe for your child.
• Keep all follow-up visits as told by your child's health care provider. This is important.
How is this prevented?
To prevent catching and spreading this virus, your child should:
• Avoid contact with people who are sick.
• Avoid contact with others by staying home and not returning to school or day care until symptoms are gone.
• Wash his or her hands often with soap and water. If soap and water are not available, your child should use a hand sanitizer. This liquid kills germs. Be sure you:
◦ Have everyone at home wash his or her hands often.
◦ Clean all surfaces and doorknobs.
• Not touch his or her face, eyes, nose, or mouth during treatment.
• Use his or her arm to cover his or her nose and mouth when coughing or sneezing.
Contact a health care provider if:
• Your child's symptoms do not lessen after 3–4 days.
Get help right away if:
• Your child's:
◦ Skin turns blue.
◦ Ribs appear to stick out during breathing.
◦ Nostrils widen during breathing.
◦ Breathing is not regular, or there are pauses during breathing. This is most likely to occur in young babies.
◦ Mouth seems dry.
• Your child:
◦ Has difficulty breathing.
◦ Makes grunting noises when breathing.
◦ Has difficulty eating or vomits often after eating.
◦ Urinates less than usual.
◦ Starts to improve but suddenly develops more symptoms.
◦ Who is younger than 3 months has a temperature of 100°F (38°C) or higher.
◦ Who is 3 months to 3 years old has a temperature of 102.2°F (39°C) or higher.
These symptoms may represent a serious problem that is an emergency. Do not wait to see if the symptoms will go away. Get medical help right away. Call your local emergency services (911 in the U.S.).
Summary
• Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) infection is a common infection in children.
• RSV spreads very easily from person to person (is very contagious). It spreads through respiratory secretions.
• Washing hands often, avoiding contact with people who are sick, and covering the nose and mouth when coughing or sneezing will help prevent this condition.
• Having your child use a cool mist humidifier, drink fluids, and avoid smoke will help support healing.
• Watch your child carefully and do not delay seeking medical care for any problems. Your child's condition can change quickly.
This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.
Document Revised: 12/20/2019 Document Reviewed: 03/05/2018
Elsevier Patient Education © 2020 Elsevier Inc.
No data available for this section
No data available for this section
No data available for this section

item.paragraph:
Schneider MD, David J: PERFORM
Event Display: PEDS Outpt Clinic Note
Authored Date: 20211130014406-0800

Assessment/Plan
_
1. Vomiting
Laurence is a 4 yo boy with 1-2 week hx of cough and rhinorrhea who developed stomach pain, nausea, vomiting, lethargy, and low-grade fever to Tmax 99F x 1 day. Discussed that he likely has a new viral illness in the setting of recent viral URI.
- Anticipatory guidance given, including that symptoms typically resolve by 7 days and that diarrhea is possible.
- Will swab for COVID-19 and Flu. Will quarantine until results are known.
- Discussed maintaining adequate hydration with clear fluids, food choices to help relieve diarrhea, and good hand washing.
- Ondansetron Rx given.
- Return to school/activities when vomiting has resolved.
- Return precautions given, including symptoms persisting beyond 7 days or development of high fever, localizing pain, anorexia, bloody stools, or bloody/bilious emesis.
Ordered:
99214 Established Patient – Level 4 Comprehensive (Moderate) 30-39 min
Influenza A/B, SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19), RSV PCR (AMB)
2. Cough
Secondary to likely viral illness. Discussed that cough can linger for about 4 weeks with a viral URI.
- Will swab for COVID-19 and RSV today.
- Supportive care discussed.
Ordered:
99214 Established Patient – Level 4 Comprehensive (Moderate) 30-39 min
Orders:
ondansetron 4 mg oral tablet, disintegrating, 0.5 tab, PO, Q8 Hours, PRN: for Nausea or Vomiting, # 5 tab(s), x 3 day(s) 12/02/2021
Chief Complaint
Vomiting/cough/lethargic
History of Present Illness
Patient is a 4-year-old boy who is brought in by mom for stomach pain, nausea, vomiting, and low-grade fever to 99 F for the past day. He was throwing up a lot last night and threw up a few times today. He has been drinking less and eating less because of this. Stomach pain is periumbilical. Mom feels like he has been more lethargic. He has had a cough and runny nose that have persisted for about 1-2 weeks. He last tested negative for COVID on 11/19/2021. He has been back to daycare since then. No diarrhea and last bowel movement was yesterday. Says he is craving chicken nuggets now.
Review of Systems
Pertinent systems were reviewed and are negative, except as stated in HPI.
Physical Exam
Vitals & Measurements
T: 98.4 °F HR: 112(Pulse) SpO2: 97%
WT: 14.4 kg
GENERAL: Alert, tired but non-toxic appearing.
HEENT: Head: Normocephalic/atraumatic.
Eyes: Conjunctivae pink without discharge. Extraocular muscles intact. Pupils equal, round, react to light and accommodation.
Ears: Tympanic membranes: normal landmarks; no erythema. B/L EACs: clear.
Nose: Clear.
Mouth/throat: No oral lesions; normal dentition.
Pharynx: No exudates or erythema.
NECK: Supple. No cervical lymphadenopathy.
LUNGS: Clear to auscultation with equal breath sounds. No wheezes, rales or rhonchi.
HEART: Regular rate and rhythm; normal S1/S2. No murmur. Normal peripheral perfusion.
ABDOMEN: Soft, non-tender, non-distended, normal bowel sounds. No hepatosplenomegaly. No masses. No guarding or rebound tenderness.
SKIN: No rashes or lesions noted.
External Vitals & Measurements
No qualifying data available.
Growth Percentiles
No qualifying data available.
Hearing and Vision Screenings
No qualifying data available.
Problem List/Past Medical History
Ongoing
Abdominal pain
Birth mark Comments: forehead
Chromosomal microduplication
Hemangioma
Prematurity
Strabismus
Historical
No qualifying data
Medications
ondansetron 4 mg oral tablet, disintegrating, 0.5 tab, PO, Q8 Hours, PRN
Vitamin D, Daily
Allergies
NKA
Social History
Home/Environment
Lives with Father, Mother, Siblings, Grandmother.
Family History
Allergies: Mother and Father.
Anxiety: Mother.
Depression: Mother.
Diabetes mellitus type 2: Sister.
Eczema: Father and Pat. GFather.
(Electronically Signed on 11/30/21 01:44 AM)
Schneider MD, David J

Schneider MD, David J: PERFORM, SIGN, VERIFY
Event Display: Instructions Patient Education
Authored Date: 20211201004332-0800

Infectious Disease
Respiratory Syncytial Virus, Pediatric
Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) infection is a common infection that occurs in childhood. RSV is similar to viruses that cause the common cold and the flu. RSV infection often is the cause of a condition known as bronchiolitis. This is a condition that causes inflammation of the air passages in the lungs (bronchioles).
RSV infection is often the reason that babies are brought to the hospital. This infection:
• Spreads very easily from person to person (is very contagious).
• Can make children sick again even if they have had it before.
• Usually affects children within the first 3 years of life but can occur at any age.
What are the causes?
This condition is caused by contact with RSV. The virus spreads through droplets from coughs and sneezes (respiratory secretions). Your child can catch it by:
• Having respiratory secretions on his or her hands and then touching his or her mouth, nose, or eyes.
• Breathing in respiratory secretions from, or coming in close physical contact with, someone who has this infection.
• Touching something that has been exposed to the virus (is contaminated) and then touching his or her mouth, nose, or eyes.
What increases the risk?
Your child may be more likely to develop severe breathing problems from RVS if he or she:
• Is younger than 2 years old.
• Was born early (prematurely).
• Was born with heart or lung disease, Down syndrome, or other medical problems that are long-term (chronic).
RVS infections are most common from the months of November to April. But they can happen any time of year.
What are the signs or symptoms?
Symptoms of this condition include:
• Breathing loudly (wheezing).
• Having brief pauses in breathing during sleep (apnea).
• Having shortness of breath.
• Coughing often.
• Having difficulty breathing.
• Having a runny nose.
• Having a fever.
• Wanting to eat less or being less active than usual.
• Having irritated eyes.
How is this diagnosed?
This condition is diagnosed based on your child's medical history and a physical exam. Your child may have tests, such as:
• A test of nasal discharge to check for RSV.
• A chest X-ray. This may be done if your child develops difficulty breathing.
• Blood tests to check for infection and dehydration getting worse.
How is this treated?
The goal of treatment is to lessen symptoms and support healing. Because RSV is a virus, usually no antibiotic medicine is prescribed. Your child may be given a medicine (bronchodilator) to open up airways in his or her lungs to help with breathing.
If your child has severe RSV infection or other health problems, he or she may need to go to the hospital. If your child:
• Is dehydrated, he or she may be given IV fluids.
• Develops breathing problems, oxygen may be given.
Follow these instructions at home:
Medicines
• Give over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your child's health care provider.
• Do not give your child aspirin because of the association with Reye's syndrome.
• Use salt–water (saline) nose drops to help keep your child's nose clear.
Lifestyle
• Keep your child away from smoke to avoid making breathing problems worse. Babies exposed to people's smoke are more likely to develop RSV.
General instructions
• Use a suction bulb as directed to remove nasal discharge and help relieve stuffed-up (congested) nose.
• Use a cool mist vaporizer in your child's bedroom at night. This is a machine that adds moisture to dry air. It helps loosen mucus.
• Have your child drink enough fluids to keep his or her urine pale yellow. Fast and heavy breathing can cause dehydration.
• Watch your child carefully and do not delay seeking medical care for any problems. Your child's condition can change quickly.
• Have your child return to his or her normal activities as told by his or her health care provider. Ask your child's health care provider what activities are safe for your child.
• Keep all follow-up visits as told by your child's health care provider. This is important.
How is this prevented?
To prevent catching and spreading this virus, your child should:
• Avoid contact with people who are sick.
• Avoid contact with others by staying home and not returning to school or day care until symptoms are gone.
• Wash his or her hands often with soap and water. If soap and water are not available, your child should use a hand sanitizer. This liquid kills germs. Be sure you:
◦ Have everyone at home wash his or her hands often.
◦ Clean all surfaces and doorknobs.
• Not touch his or her face, eyes, nose, or mouth during treatment.
• Use his or her arm to cover his or her nose and mouth when coughing or sneezing.
Contact a health care provider if:
• Your child's symptoms do not lessen after 3–4 days.
Get help right away if:
• Your child's:
◦ Skin turns blue.
◦ Ribs appear to stick out during breathing.
◦ Nostrils widen during breathing.
◦ Breathing is not regular, or there are pauses during breathing. This is most likely to occur in young babies.
◦ Mouth seems dry.
• Your child:
◦ Has difficulty breathing.
◦ Makes grunting noises when breathing.
◦ Has difficulty eating or vomits often after eating.
◦ Urinates less than usual.
◦ Starts to improve but suddenly develops more symptoms.
◦ Who is younger than 3 months has a temperature of 100°F (38°C) or higher.
◦ Who is 3 months to 3 years old has a temperature of 102.2°F (39°C) or higher.
These symptoms may represent a serious problem that is an emergency. Do not wait to see if the symptoms will go away. Get medical help right away. Call your local emergency services (911 in the U.S.).
Summary
• Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) infection is a common infection in children.
• RSV spreads very easily from person to person (is very contagious). It spreads through respiratory secretions.
• Washing hands often, avoiding contact with people who are sick, and covering the nose and mouth when coughing or sneezing will help prevent this condition.
• Having your child use a cool mist humidifier, drink fluids, and avoid smoke will help support healing.
• Watch your child carefully and do not delay seeking medical care for any problems. Your child's condition can change quickly.
This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.
Document Revised: 12/20/2019 Document Reviewed: 03/05/2018
Elsevier Patient Education © 2020 Elsevier Inc.
